`CancelEvent` is the last event transmitted by the engine as a signal that logging is over. I've used it here to signal that the thread can terminate, so when `thread.join()` waits it doesn't always time out because otherwise there would be no end to this `_watch_logs` function. We can also use an `Event` object to [signal completion](https://docs.python.org/3/library/threading.html#id1:~:text=A%20thread%20can%20be%20flagged%20as,signalling%20mechanism%20such%20as%20an%20Event.) though, if there is hesitation to go this route.

```python
branch_args = []

# Note the * character. This has no effect if the list is empty.
self.run('git', 'init', *branch_args)
```

The question one wants to have the answer to is what genes are covered by this analysis.

This is all just a workaround for not having variant type in the panel definition, the way e.g. PanelApp do. Each gene should just have a tag with what variant types are considered clinical in that gene: SV, STR etc.

The ideal end result would be a join with a cohort level chanjo report, so that a gene that does not sequence well - gets low coverage on multiple analyses - is not on the list even if it is in the panel. In gcs actor scheduler, `actor_scheduling_canceled` is set true only when gcs' `ClusterTaskManager` ReplyCancelled with `SCHEDULING_CANCELLED_UNSCHEDULABLE`.  If we call `CancelActorInScheduling` in this case, we will remove the task from `ClusterTaskManager`'s pending queue (via calling`GcsActorManager::RemovePendingActor()`). Then when we call `work_it = work_queue.erase(work_it);` (at line 122 in `ClusterTaskManager::ScheduleAndDispatchTasks()`), we will run into a `double free` fatal failure. 

To solve this problem, we can just call `work_it = work_queue.erase(work_it);` before calling `ReplyCancelled(*, rpc::RequestWorkerLeaseReply::SCHEDULING_CANCELLED_UNSCHEDULABLE, *)`. But then we call `GcsActorManager::RemovePendingActor()`, we will not find the task from the pending queue. Then we will go to `GcsActorScheduler::CancelOnLeasing()` and get a RAY_CHECK failure (line 229 in `GcsActorScheduler::CancelOnLeasing()`), because this worker lease has never been sent out.

Anyway, this logic is complicated, and I feel the current change is the most straightforward and easy-to-understand way.

```suggestion
You configure OpenSSL to use the FIPS provider using the FIPS configuration in OpenSSL config. You can modify the default configuration file to include FIPS related config or create a new configuration file point to it using the environment variable,`OPENSSL_CONF`. For example `OPENSSL_CONF="/path/to/fips/enabled/openssl/config/openssl.cnf`:
```

I see two issues with this.  I think this is a pretty costly operation since it recursively re-computes these array dimensions making this function have something like an `O(N*2)` complexity or worse.  

Maybe you could re-structure this as something like the following and do this in two stages.

```python
@to_tuple
def enumerate_depth_and_dimensions(data, depth):
    """
    Yields 2-tuples of depth and length of element at that depth
    """
    if not is_non_string_iterable(v):
        return
    
    yield depth, len(data)

    for item in data:
        yield from enumerate_depth_and_dimensions(item, depth + 1)

def get_array_dimensions(data):
    depths_and_dimensions = get_depths_and_dimensions(data)
    # re-form as a dictionary with `depth` as key, and all of the dimensions found at that depth.
    grouped_by_depth = toolz.groupby(operator.itemgetter(0), depths_and_dimensions)
    # validate that there is only one dimension for any given depth.
    invalid_depths = tuple(depth for depth, dimensions in grouped_by_depths.items() if len(set(dimensions)) != 1)
    if invalid_depths:
        raise ValidationError("...")
    dimensions = tuple(toolz.first(set(dimensions)) for depth, dimensions in sorted(grouped_by_depth.items()))
```

This is of course untested psuedo-code and should be cleaned up for readabiltity but it should demonstrate the following algorithem.

- walk the full trie producing an enumeration of each element by it's depth and dimension.
- group all of the values together by depth and verify that each depth only has a single dimension, raising an error if this is not true.
- collate the dimension data sorted by depth

The error messaging could be improved if the enumeration of the depths and dimensions also contained the path to that part of the data which would allow for better error reporting.

Well this is the interesting bit with the green band correction. The location of the AHI/FCI green bands has two effects:
1. Vegetation appears brownish.
2. Barren appears reddish.

The green color of vegetation can be boosted with a higher contribution from the NIR channel. Similarly, the reddishness of barren is reduced with an increasing contribution from the NIR channel. However, more contribution from NIR is required to get the proper color of barren, compared to vegetation. Hence, I came up with the solution of increasing the contribution from NIR with decreasing NDVI.

Below are a few examples using the default hybrid green method, with decreasing contribution from the NIR channel. As you can see the color of Australia starts to get too red when the color of vegetation start to look realistic. I also included the result of the NDVIHybridGreen (with just qualitative tuning) and VIIRS RGB from NASA worldview as reference.

OK, so there's a lot going on here, hidden behind this `str` hack, which is a bit voodoo and not fully correct (or rather, only correct because of certain facts about how `json.dumps` works).

Here's the deal:

Python2 is very loose about converting character strings to byte arrays and back as needed, and it uses the interpreter's default encoding to do so. 

So in Python2 you can write bytes to a file opened in text mode or a string to a file opened in binary mode, and it works. 

But Python3 is stricter: you must write a string to a file opened in text mode, and bytes to a file opened in binary mode.

(best way to get a feel for all this is to try it in a REPL).

Now, `json.dumps()` always returns a byte array (except in some weird circumstances on Python2, which we can ignore). This is because JSON itself supports encoding all non-ASCII characters using `\uXXXX` sequences. So any valid JSON can always be represented in pure ASCII, so there is no need to worry about encodings.

When you cast it to a `str`, on Python2 this is a no-op (`dumps` already returned a byte array), and on Python3 this turns the result of `dumps` from a byte array to a character string using whatever the interpreter default encoding is, and then `fp.write()` immediately decodes it using the same default encoding. This is obviously wonky. 

Now, in practice all these encodings and decodings are no-ops, because `dumps` returned ASCII, but it's still confusing and unnecessary. It's not obvious to the casual reader what is going on, or why it works out to be correct in practice.

Instead of casting to `str`, you can just open the file in binary mode: `wb`. Then you can write the result of `dumps` directly.

`PriorityQueue::GetHead()` returns the highest priority message. Instead, we want to start with the lowest priority messages.

So it finds the tail of the lowest priority message, then returns the next message, which should be the highest priority message (due to the circularly linked list).

One of the problems we have here is that there are several different user intents that we are trying to map into a single function - Create, partial or full Update and Replace.

For Create and Replace, flattening the parameters make sense. For update, you have update-based-on-resource-state (I need to look at the state of the resource before I determine if and what updates I want to make) and partial updates (I just want to change this subset of properties on the resource).

It sounds like the only thing the service supports is effectively the "replace" (not partial update) scenario. This makes it much more likely to, as @xiangyan99 points out, the user having to get the resource, modify it locally and then put it back (the service didn't allow me to send just the value I wanted to mutate). 

In these cases, the best option is often to take both the full model and the flattened parameters. Any flattened value would override what's in the model. Yes, you can specify values twice (in the model and as a direct parameter). Which is somewhat problematic. But it generally works. And is relatively easy to explain.

It is not safe to use [`lstrip`](https://docs.python.org/3.8/library/stdtypes.html#str.lstrip):

> `str.lstrip([chars])`
> The `chars` argument is a string specifying the set of characters to be removed.

So this can cause unwanted stripping of `hsp`:

```
>>> "https://hsp.myorg.com".lstrip('https://')
'.myorg.com'
```

This custom field bypasses the dbapi implicit conversion by treating the sqlite field as a simple string when serializing/deserializing, and doing the parsing ourselves.

Na it's not cmake.  It's easy to have real clean cmake. It's all complicated because there are so many people with broken systems or compilers or whatever.  You can make it so simple.  But then someone posts "hey, I'm building on $some_weird_linux_distro_or_whatever and I'm getting this linker error saying libpng is missing some symbol".  Or they are complaining about something else that is actually broken on their system.  For instance, Anaconda includes these copies of x11 header files right, which for a lot of people end up in their compiler's include search path.  And Anaconda has helpfully edited those headers to be different from the real x11 headers, so if those get included instead the world breaks.   I could go on and on.

Anyway, those people complain to me though.  "Hey, dlib isn't building?!".  So I add some logic to the dlib cmake scripts to detect whatever is wrong with their system and work around it.  Like all that logic in there for testing if libpng is *really* there or not.

The only way this whole mess could be resolved was if applications *never ever* depended on libraries they are not themselves building.  And the building of those libraries would have to be controlled by one unified build system.  But that's a tall ask because everyone then turns around and says "hey, I hate the build times.  Can't the $(os, package system, whatever) provide precompiled binaries instead?" which is a desire 100% contradictory to resolving this problem given the nature of the One Definition Rule and the diversity of compile and build settings.

Then you top this all off with proprietary vendor libraries.  Like IntelMKL or anything CUDA related.  Which you can't build.  They have to be somehow pulled in as binaries because that's the only things those vendors will give out.  And those binaries have OS dependencies and even driver level dependencies. 

So IDK.  Any language that wants to do anything with hardware acceleration has to grapple with this.  I guess in the dream world there would be BLAS, LAPACK, and CUDA built into compilers by default.  Which is just saying that we would foist the problem of that stuff onto compiler and OS vendors and they would just deal with it.

Okay I thought more about this and realized that partitioning is irrelevant to whether we have `--mypy-interpreter-constraints` or not. We can always allow users to set this.

But should we? If they set it, it's very possible that we will end up needing to set `PEX_IGNORE_ERRORS`, which may cause MyPy to complain that there are missing type hints for libraries, even if they should normally exist. This is really subtle; it's not at all obvious to an end user that `--mypy-interpreter-constraints` must match their requirements's compatibility. And that would be really error prone to try to hardcode it to match, imo.

On the other hand, idk if it's useful to expose this functionality for some reason, that you really don't want Pants to autoconfigure?

The implementation seems fine to me. Invariants might not be hashable, but that seems unlikely.

`validateInvariants` is a public method with a documented signature, though, and I'm not sure I like adding a public argument to that. The documentation and introspection by things like IDEs will disagree. I've seen this handled in two different ways. One is to name the argument with a leading underscore, indicating its for internal usage.
```suggestion
    def validateInvariants(self, obj, errors=None, _seen=None):
```

The other is to move the real work to an internal function:
```suggestion
    def validateInvariants(self, obj, errors=None):
        return self.__validateInvariants(obj, errors, set())
```
This has the advantage of not needing to check that `seen` is None at the start of the function. (We'd use `base.__validateInvariants(obj, errors, seen)` below too, of course.)

It's possible, (but not likely), that someone has subclassed `InterfaceClass` and overridden `validateInvariants` or used `@interfacemethod` to write their own `validateInvariants` method. The current implementation, as well as the first alternate form, could break such classes. I think the second alternate form (internal implementation) would be safe, but could wind up bypassing the custom methods.

I think +1 is kind of like a ad-hoc patch for your specific test data. The real problem is:
1. You can never know if target_col(e.g. "value") is ahead of feature_col(e.g. "extra_features") or not
2. The order in your scaler is [feature_col, target_col]
3. That's why the order is wrong!


Solution is:
1. change the order in your scaler to [target_col, feature_col] (just like what we do in TSDataset)
2. get the scaler_index in roll rather than unscale (just like what we do in TSDataset)
3. scaler_index can be set to `[self.target_col.index(feature_col[i]) for i in range(len(feature_col))] ` (just like what we do in TSDataset)

This is a ASGI middleware that can be used in starlette.

```python
class ASGIMiddleware:
    def __init__(app: ASGIApp, p0: str, p1: int) -> None:
        ...
    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send) -> None:
        ...
```

This also can be used in starlette.

```python
def asgi_middleware(app, p0: str, p1: int) -> ASGIApp:
    def _asgi(scope, receive, send) -> None:
        ...
    return _asgi
```

But this can't be used in starlette. Even though `P` passes the type check you're giving now.

```python
class P:
    def __init__(self) -> None: ...
    def __call__(self, scope, receive, send) -> None: ...
```

To be consistent with everything else in the SDK that wraps async transport by exposing `__aenter__`, `__aexit__`, and `close`. This PR does add an `async with` example to the README, and it's okay to use credentials that way. Every other credential's `__aenter__` invokes its transport's `__aenter__`.

The awkwardness for `ChainedTokenCredential.__aenter__` is that if it opens sessions for N credentials, N-1 of them may never be used, at some cost dependent on the HTTP client's implementation. These sessions will all be closed by `__aexit__`, but I thought it unnecessary to open them given that our async transports will do so as needed.

Updating my comments from above now that I've seen the latest version:

If I understand correctly, we have a new set of Windower classes which (1) use a new internal function (_supercrop_starts) instead of mne.events_from_annotations to identify events, (2) use 'crop'/'supercrop' language instead of 'windows' in the variable names and docstring and (3) set the default behaviour to drop_samples=False (instead of requiring the user to set this explicitly).

1. Before I forget, I think docstrings should start with a one-line short description on the same line as the first """: see https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#documentation-strings
2. Why did we chose to use a new internal function instead of the existing MNE functions? Personally, I think the closer we stay to the MNE way of doing things, the simpler it will be to maintain the package. Also, people already familiar with MNE don't have to learn a new way of extracting windows.
3.  Do you think we should keep the crop/supercrop-specific language? My worry is that since this class is central to the package, and these concepts are relatively niche, they might make the understanding of the library more difficult. As far as I understand, supercrop could just be replaced by window here as there is no subdivision into crops happening at this level.
4. I understand that you guys' use case is to extract a window that covers the last "missed" samples in each event. I still believe that this default behaviour can be problematic in some cases (as the opposite behaviour can be problematic in others). I liked our previous solution to this which was to force the user to provide drop_samples - what do you think?

Based on early conversations about this approach, I was looking for a place where "two copies" of a module were held, one for use by `ddtrace` internals and the other for use by user code.

Here's the sequence of events after this change as I currently understand it. I'd appreciate if you could tell me where I'm wrong @P403n1x87.

1. ddtracepy runs `import threading`. `'threading' in sys.modules`, `threading` names bound into a namespace internal to ddtracepy
2. ddtracepy starts a thread
3. ddtracepy calls `cleanup_loaded_modules`. `'threading' not in sys.modules`, `threading` names still bound in internal namespace
4. user code runs `import gevent.monkey; gevent.monkey.patch_all()`. does this affect the running thread in any way?
5. user code runs `import threading`. because `'threading' not in sys.modules`, the module is fetched from disk and names are bound into a user-code namespace
6. from this point on, names from the `threading` module in both user code and ddtracepy code reference the copy of the module loaded in user code (and monkeypatched by gevent). objects (like threads) instantiated at step 2 continue to exist as instances of names from the module loaded in step 1.

In this sequence, "two copies" of the `threading` module aren't bound to different namespaces at the same moment. Instead, objects created via the access of names from `threading` existing at the same moment could be from either the pre- or post-monkeypatch names. That implies that our library actually needs to create all such objects that it will ever need before cleaning up its copy of `threading`.

Rather than catch-all passing the splatted `kwargs` through to the request body, it'd probably be better to be explicit on this argument; perhaps something like `body: Dict[str, Any]` or `config`, etc? Otherwise, folks will need to manually serialize/deserialize their request between the dictionary form mentioned in the API docs and Python argument form, which in many cases might involve some ugly parsing. Additionally, this may swallow more arguments than we'd like in the future, if we augment this method signature -- it'd be difficult to add, say, a `timeout` argument to this method without running into version conflicts where old versions try to use the `timeout` value as part of the actual request body!

Eg. by writing `**kwargs` here, you're only accepting arguments of the form `.create_subscription(..., foo='foo', bar='bar'), rather than the more JSON-API form of `{'foo': 'foo', 'bar': 'bar'}`.

That makes sense to me. I have converted all of the tests to use the relative error, as you describe above. 

There is one remaining issue that seems to be related to the gradient descent results that affects both the `PoissonRegressor` results and the `LogisticRegression` results.

First, When running the test with a relative error tolerance of `1e-3`, it typically does not pass for the `PoissonRegressor` with gradient descent (Newton's method and LBFGS achieve the same results). I have spent a while debugging the data creation functions, but have not been able to reliably get the test to pass with the `1e-3` relative error tolerance. I suspect that this is due to having a large number of features, as increasing `N / p` from `5` to `10` does decrease the error, and potentially due to a non-convex loss function for this data leading to difficulty for the gradient descent algorithm. `scikit-learn` defaults to LBFGS for `sklearn.linear_model.PoissonRegressor` with no alternative option. For the `LogisticRegression`, the test of the relative error of the coefficients using `1e-3` is similarly too large to pass (as the test is written, it is approx. `0.14`) when using gradient descent. It converges to the `scikit-learn` results for both Newton's method and LBFGS.

Maybe the best strategy is to remove gradient descent from the tests and supply some sort of convergence warning when using vanilla GD?

@nsheaps A follow up from our previous discussions about replacing this `try/except` block with checking the config for metrics being enabled:

It turns out there are two different ways to enable the current statsd metrics (and you cannot set both at the same time):  
1. `metrics.tagging` enables the tagged metrics (described [here](https://github.snooguts.net/reddit/baseplate.spec/blob/master/component-apis/metrics-tagged.md))
2. `metrics.namespace` enables non-tagged metrics (described [here](https://github.snooguts.net/reddit/baseplate.spec/blob/master/component-apis/metrics.md)).

Searching in sourcegraph, it looks like there are approx. 82 repos that set `metrics.namespace` and 20 repos that set `metric.tagging`.  For reference, here are the sourcegraph queries I ran:
```
repo:reddit-service-* repo:contains.file(requirements.txt) file:ini metrics.tagging select:repo
repo:reddit-service-* repo:contains.file(requirements.txt) file:ini metrics.namespace select:repo
```

Since the goal is to enable Prometheus metrics alongside the current statsd metrics (until wavefront is turned off), then we need to enable Prometheus when either `metrics.tagging` or `metrics.namespace` are set.

Another consideration is to create a new config option specifically for Prometheus.  It would be nice to have a separate config for the Prometheus metrics to keep it untangled from the current metric configs options, however there wouldn't be a way to turn on Prometheus automatically without updating the `.ini` file which would make upgrades not backwards compatible.  But I'm open to a discussion either way.

I think what's happening is that `sphinx-gallery` generates intermediate `.rst` files from the example scripts and puts them in `sphinx/source/auto_examples`, making them look just like normal RST source files.  When sphinx itself runs, it doesn't realize those files were just auto-generated a moment ago and so it creates "edit on github" links assuming that those RST files exist on GH as well.

Looks like it isn't a trivial fix to make the links point to the original .py files -- MetPy handles it with a custom template: https://github.com/Unidata/MetPy/commit/8f1783af3bdb2b3d664faf6d3eeb8af9024fed85. If nobody is eager, I'll volunteer to try to get it working in a separate PR.

Here is a code testing the differences between Pytorch CELU and the implementation (with corrected parenthesis) I provided.

```
import numpy as np
import torch

def onnx_celu(input_data, alpha=1.0):
    positive_input = np.maximum(0, input_data)
    negative_input = np.minimum(0, alpha * (np.exp(input_data / alpha) - 1))
    output_data = positive_input + negative_input
    return output_data

def torch_celu(input_data, alpha=1.0):
    return torch.nn.CELU(alpha=alpha)(torch.Tensor(input_data)).numpy()

input_data = np.random.randn(1, 2, 3).astype('float32')
alpha = 2

assert (onnx_celu(input_data, alpha) == torch_celu(input_data, alpha)).all()

```

It is fully asynchronous (at least I hope so :) ):
- Each time the training iteration function is run, we make sure that each worker has at least n `sample` requests "in flight", so that each worker is basically always sampling in the background (async). We never wait for any worker to complete, but only ever collect what's already done anyways.
- Only those requests that are done are returned here (with an optional timeout) and the driver script can immediately processed the next item (which is requesting policy updates, also asynchronous). So the very first time, you run this, there will be nothing returned (after the timeout) as you just kicked off the background sampling.

Basic Op Registration requires:
- Parse Attrs

Stateful Op Registration requires:
- Parse Attrs

- Infer Shape
- Create Op State
- Forward Stateful

One of the goal of this pull request is to be able to read credentials directly out of an environment variable; `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS` contains the _path_ to a file containing the credentials, not the credentials itself.

This is not supported natively by the Google SDK (I can find the related issues in their repository, if needed), but other consumers may provide this, for example the [Terraform Google provider](https://registry.terraform.io/providers/hashicorp/google/latest/docs/guides/provider_reference#using-terraform-cloud). Being able to read the credentials directly out of an environment variable would open up the possibility to use sops in context where it's not possible to read the credentials otherwise.

Hence, the deliberate use of this `GOOGLE_CREDENTIALS` environment variable, like the Google provider for Terraform does.

```
    def create_topic(self, prefix, conf=None):
        """
        Creates a new topic with this cluster.

        :param str prefix: topic name
        :param dict conf: additions/overrides to topic configuration.
        :returns: The topic's name
        :rtype: str
        """
        if self._admin is None:
            self._admin = AdminClient(self.client_conf())

        name = prefix + str(uuid1())
        future_topic = self._admin.create_topics([NewTopic(name,
                                                           **self._topic_conf(conf))])

        future_topic.get(name).result()
        return name
```

I can't think of a case where I want an `exists` on the target file but wouldn't want a regular hash on the input parameters. Hence the specific naming of `hash_target_files` defaulting to True. I hesitate to add an 'exists' hash algorithm to input files because I don't have a case where I've ever needed to do that. 

For what it's worth I've been using the `hash_target_files` syntax in some other projects and it feels initiative to me.

I would suggest to seperate the reindexing of the snapshots also depending on the type of the "value". By that one could more easily switch from a single-index network to a multi-index network without loosing the time-dependent data, e.g.         
```
 n = pypsa.Network("examples/scigrid-de/scigrid-with-load-gen-trafos") 
 n.snapshot_weightings["objective"] = 2. 
 multi_index =  pd.MultiIndex.from_product([['first'], n.snapshots])
 n.set_snapshots(multi_index)
```
One would loose the information about the objective weightings. I would suggest to change the setting of the snapshots to 
`def set_snapshots(self, value):
        """
        Set the snapshots and reindex all time-dependent data.

        This will reindex all pandas.Panels of time-dependent data; NaNs are filled
        with the default value for that quantity.

        Parameters
        ----------
        snapshots : list or pandas.Index
            All time steps.

        Returns
        -------
        None
        """
        if isinstance(value, pd.MultiIndex):
            assert value.nlevels == 2, "Maximally two levels of MultiIndex supported"
            self._snapshots = value.rename(['period', 'snapshot'])

            self.snapshot_weightings = (
                self.snapshot_weightings.reindex(self._snapshots, level=1, fill_value=1.))
        else:
            self._snapshots = pd.Index(value, name='snapshot')

            self.snapshot_weightings = (
                self.snapshot_weightings.reindex(self._snapshots, fill_value=1.))

        for component in self.all_components:
            pnl = self.pnl(component)
            attrs = self.components[component]["attrs"]

            for k,default in attrs.default[attrs.varying].iteritems():
                if isinstance(value, pd.MultiIndex):
                    pnl[k] = pnl[k].reindex(self._snapshots, level=1).fillna(default)
                else:
                    pnl[k] = pnl[k].reindex(self._snapshots).fillna(default)`

        so before step 3, we construct partition_wwid from boot_disk_wwid

You are correct, one can still trigger a `MemoryError` if one wants to instantiate the lookup-table as a numpy array. However, instantiating the lookup-table is not required to access the functionality of relabeling. The same functionality is provided (without memory issues) using the `__getitem__` and `__call__` interfaces.

Example:
```
labels =  np.array([1, 1e16], dtype=np.int64)

# 1. call relabel_sequential
relab, fw, inv = relabel_sequential(labels) # does not allocate tons of memory

# 2. use the returned forward transform to relabel the array again using fancy indexing:
_relab = fw[labels] # does not allocate tons of memory ... is handled by __getitem__

# 3. use the returned forward transform to relabel the array again using call interdace:
_relab = fw(labels) # does not allocate tons of memory ... is handled by __call__

# 4. turn the returned forward transform into a numpy array 
fw_lut = np.narray(fw) # this will now try and allocate a huge array !
```

Use case `4.` is the only one where we still are likely to get `MemoryErrors` if we have large values in the input array. So in that sense one can still potentially hit this problem in the current code. However, this functionality is exclusively for backwards compatibility, in case anyone has code that used the returned transformations as an array (and not just for fancy indexing) for whatever reason. 
We provide the functionality for relabeling another array using the `fw` or `inv` transforms using methods `2.,3.`.

I agree that this is not immediately obvious. To address this I have the following suggestions:
1. spell out very clearly in the docstring that `np.array(fw)` is not recommended, or
1. raise a warning in `__array__`  that states that this is not recommended and for backwards compatibility only, or
1. remove the `__array__` method altogether, which may be a breaking change but will likely not affect a lot of use cases.

In fact there is several things here.

Now basically, when you want to override an option, you implement `_test_parameters_overrides` in the relevant test class, and optionally `_test_fallback_fn` when you want to modify what default value will be given to any option that does not have a value (from environment variables or config file, in the case of integration tests.

Now, I saw a lot of code that would override parameters in a given provider, but with these parameters that are not formerly exposed options. For instance, a lot of `api_endpoint` parameters that cannot be set by command line, environment variables or configuration files. A logic is just hardcoded in the provider class to allow to modify the value during tests. At least I guesse e there is no clue that there were another value beside the default one during tests.

I do not like this, because you push code in the provider class that is here only to make the test be runnable. In theory, you should not modify your codebase to test it, you have mocks for that. And here `_test_parameters_overrides` and `_test_fallback_fn` are these kind of mocks.

I took a really opinionated approach. I decided to remove all codes from provider classes that were obviously here only for the tests. And to not provide any particular logic to modify provider parameters for tests only. Currently, any parameter that needs to be overriden during tests is required to be a real configuration parameter, so settable through command line, environment variable or configuration file.

If someone needs to relaunch integration tests against a real test API that does not provide a configurable `api_endpoint`, he will need to implement the logic on the provider parser, the provider class and the provider test.

I know this is a quite radical approach, but I think that being consistent about the code of the provider toward the code of the test will improve the code quality.

The following PR:
- https://github.com/python/cpython/pull/10307

was introduced in Python 3.9, and fixes class detection when they are nested in other classes.

Up until now, it would still fail with the following:

```python
import inspect

class Model:
    a = 1

class Model:
    a = 2

print(Model.a)
#> 2
print(inspect.getsource(Model))
#> class Model:
#>     a = 1
```

This got improved with the following PR (it will now pick the last class definition):
- https://github.com/python/cpython/pull/106815

But still can get it wrong if you're doing something like:

```python
if False:
    class Model:
        a = 1
else:
    class Model:
        a = 2
# Which really is a uncommon edge case
```

> when it gets the wrong source, does it lead to actual exceptions being raised, or does it just retrieve the docstrings incorrectly?

It will not raise currently, as applying field docstrings in a safe way, but I could maybe raise if we encounter unexpected fields (I'll have to see if it's doable when inheritance is being used).

So to summarize, all of your conditions should indeed be met, except:
- You are using Python<=3.9
- it fails gracefully (can be fixed as I said)

I thought I had understood that foreign keys can, at least in theory, also point to some other non-ID non-PK column. There's a call-out concerning that in the CSVW context:
https://www.w3.org/TR/2015/REC-tabular-metadata-20151217/#foreign-key-reference-between-schemas, Section 5.5, which as far as I understand we inherit indirectly, states

1. Ignore only requests in metrics exporter
2. Ignore requests in Azure Exporter
3. Ignore all requests from OpenCensus (including other ext)

IMO as a user, when I instrument with OC, I would want none of the requests from OC to be tracked. So if I were to instrument with multiple exporters (stackdriver, azure, etc.) I wouldn't want those requests (if any) to be tracked in my metrics. With that being said, opencensus-ext-azure is the only extension that utilizes "requests", so as you mentioned, adding logic for disabling checks for everything in that extension would be needed.

So, there were a couple models here that I considered. You could just
replace the `onFinish` continuation with “returns `Promise<void>`”,
which I presume is what you’re suggesting. That works, but I thought it
was a bit confusing to mix callbacks and promises. The alternative that
I’d be more interested in is requiring the function to just return an
array of `Promise<{item: Item, data: Data}>`—either that or an
early-bound array of `{item: Item, data: Promise<Data>}`. In some ways
this felt a bit more restrictive because `requestData` has to explicitly
farm out promises for each item, but of course it’s equally flexible
assuming that you don’t do unexpected things like call the `onLoad`
callback multiple times. This also has a nice way to reject individual
promises, which would be a strong argument if `DataLoaderBehavior` had a
notion of failed load states.

```suggestion
def is_empty_function(fn: typing.Callable) -> bool:
    """
    Returns true if the function is empty.
    """
    import dis
    fst, snd, *rest = [i for i in dis.Bytecode(fn)] 
    is_load_none = fst.opname == "LOAD_CONST" and fst.argval == None 
    is_return = snd.opname == "RETURN_VALUE" 
    return is_load_none and is_return and not rest 
```

This way of starting the timer is indeed intentional. I think we want the ticks to be emitted at fixed rate vs fixed delay between ticks.

The tick receivers generally use it for tracking durations or  timeout (in seconds). e.g., expiring address resolver cache entries after some time.

For example, if there is a task/operation that holds the execution for a while (e.g., flash erase), and the raw time value does jump ahead more than a second, I think we do want to emit multiple ticks back to back (since in terms of actual time, really multiple seconds have passed).

BTW, `Timer` does work correctly (fires immediately) if we schedule it with a time instance in the past (relative to `now`). Method `Timer::DoesFireBefore()` handles this.

**Design note:** Rather than implementing the `BatcherInterface` with async/background batching being an implementation detail, where the `AsyncBatcher` ends up mostly being a proxy to the base batcher, I'm wondering if it would be cleaner to lift the backgrounding of the batcher out of the abstraction, e.g. where the background thread and queue are defined outside of the batcher, and `batcher.next()` is called in the background thread. This is a common design pattern of separating the operation (in this case batch preparation) from the execution and operator-to-operator communication model (background thread and queue), and should make it easier to change/add more operations and tweak the execution/communication model.

If we refactor the batch producer (`batch_blocks`) code to be a chained sequence of `Iterable[Data] -> Iterable[Data]` generators, I think we can more cleanly separate the data processing code (e.g. prefetching, batching, formatting, etc.) from the execution/orchestration code (serial vs. background thread vs. threadpool vs. actor, and iterator vs. queue); this is similar to the [Torch `IterDataPipe` paradigm](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/49440), and they essentially had the same motivations as us. We could then have an `Iterable[ray.ObjectRef[Block]] -> Iterable[Block]` (pre)fetcher, an `Iterable[Block] -> Iterable[Batch]` batcher, and an `Iterable[Batch] -> Iterable[Batch]` formatter.

With --> representing a simple iterable feed (no queueing/buffering), the status quo (no batcher background thread) is
```
producer -(plasma_queue)-> fetcher --> batcher --> formatter --> consumer
```
where it's single-threaded (except for the Ray Core object store producer, which is multithreaded) and serial. This PR would be changing this to
```
producer -(plasma_queue)-> fetcher --> [batcher --> formatter] -(queue)-> consumer
```
where everything in `[]` is in a background thread. Explicitly representing this in the `batch_blocks` code would make it easier to tweak this going forward, e.g. moving the fetcher into the background thread as well, doing some of the `fetcher --> batcher --> formatter` chain in parallel over a threadpool, etc.

>Is it guaranteed that after all callbacks are invoked after restoration, the pull requests are cancelled?

The pull request will be cancelled by higher-level code around task execution or fulfilling `ray.get()` or `ray.wait()`, but we don't want the pull requests to be cancelled right after restoration since we still need to pull the object.

>Otherwise it is possible is_being_restored is still true after all of callbacks are completed right?

There should be at most one `restore_spilled_object_` call, and therefore at most one callback, per pull request (due to our deduping on `request.is_being_restored`); after this callback executes, `request.is_being_restored` will be `false`, so the next time `TryToMakeObjectLocal` executes, it will pull the object.So I don't think that there's any way for `request.is_being_restored` to be true after the callback has completed.

For `error` in both `WWW-Authenticate` and HTTP body, I have not seen any hard requirements, but the RFC specifies that 401 can be used, and `error` must be in the response's body. It doesn't said explicitly that both can be used in same time, though..

Introspect RFC:
```
   If the protected resource uses OAuth 2.0 client credentials to
   authenticate to the introspection endpoint and its credentials are
   invalid, the authorization server responds with an HTTP 401
   (Unauthorized) as described in Section 5.2 of OAuth 2.0 [RFC6749].
```

OAuth2 5.2 section:
```
   The authorization server responds with an HTTP 400 (Bad Request)
   status code (unless specified otherwise) and includes the following
   parameters with the response:
       error
         invalid_client
               (..).  The authorization server MAY
               return an HTTP 401 (Unauthorized) status code to indicate
               which HTTP authentication schemes are supported.  If the
               client attempted to authenticate via the "Authorization"
               request header field, the authorization server MUST
               respond with an HTTP 401 (Unauthorized) status code and
               include the "WWW-Authenticate" response header field
               matching the authentication scheme used by the client.

   (..)
   The parameters are included in the entity-body of the HTTP response
   using the "application/json" media type as defined by [RFC4627].  (..)
```

And then Bearer RFC6750:
```
3.  The WWW-Authenticate Response Header Field
   (..)
   All challenges defined by this specification MUST use the auth-scheme
   value "Bearer".  This scheme MUST be followed by one or more
   auth-param values.
   (..)
   If the protected resource request included an access token and failed
   authentication, the resource server SHOULD include the "error"
   attribute to provide the client with the reason why the access
   request was declined.
```

I think this 401/Authenticate topic deserves changes more in-depth, and this PR could be a short term solution to progress forward.
In the future, we should handle all areas of the (at least) three RFCs, mostly because we don't support everything (bearer authentication vs client authentication; sending `the authentication scheme used by the client`; `scope`/`realm`; and so on and so forth)


To summarize, examples in Bearer's RFC shows no HTTP body, the OAuth2's RFC
 require the error in the HTTP body. OAuthlib-2 send errors in the HTTP body, what should do OAuthlib-3 ? :-)

```suggestion
    Situations non prises en compte
    - en contrat aidé,

    - jeunes suivis par l'Aide Sociale à l'Enfance.​

    entity = Individu

        "https://programme-depart-1825.com/eligibilite/",

        ]

    def formula(individu, period, parameters):
        criteres_age = parameters(period).prestations.depart1825.age
        age = individu('age', period)

        eligibilite_age = (criteres_age.minimum <= age) * (age <= criteres_age.maximum)

        etudiant_boursier = (individu('activite', period) == TypesActivite.etudiant) * individu('boursier', period)
        alternant = individu('alternant', period)

        plafond_ressources = plafond_ressources.base + 2 * max_(0, nbptr - 1) * plafond_ressources.par_demi_part_supplementaire
        
        ressources = individu.foyer_fiscal('rfr', period.n_2)

        eligibilite_financiere = ressources <= plafond_ressources

        return eligibilite_age * (eligibilite_statut + eligibilite_financiere)
```

I've been trying to think about how to remove the duplication w/ parsing the `Version` and re-raising a new exception. It's not the End Of The World to duplicate it, but not ideal, e.g. if you change the error message and forget to update the other.

One way you could de-duplicate is like this:

```python
is_caret = req[0] and "^"
# ~= is an acceptable default operator; however, ~ is not, and IS NOT the same as ~=
is_tilde = req[0] == "~" and req[1] != "="
if is_caret or is_tilde:
     try:
          version = Version(req[1])
     except ..:
          ...
     max_ver = get_max_caret(version) if is_caret else get_max_tilde(version)
     min_ver = req[1:]
     pep440_reqs.append(f">={min_ver},<{max_ver}")
else:
    pep440_reqs.append(req if req[0] in valid_specifiers else f"=={req}")
```

Note that this allows you to simplify the `get_max_tilde` and `get_max_caret` functions to take fewer arguments.

I think really the problem here has stemmed from the fact we have been passing around the `return_all_layers` argument as a constructor parameter and not a runtime `forward` parameter (or as a separate method on a `Contextualizer` subclass of `Seq2SeqEncoder`.

Is the right API here this:

```python
class Seq2SeqEncoder

class Contextualizer(Seq2SeqEncoder):

	# Idea 1:
    def forward(sequence: torch.Tensor[batch, sequence, embedding],
				return_all_layers = False) -> Union[torch.Tensor[batch, sequence, embedding], List[of the same thing]]

	# this way all Contextualizers can still function as `Seq2SeqEncoders` by default,
	# which is useful for training and perhaps for downstream use, say if you wanted to
	# fine tune an encoder and didn't want to do an elmo mixture. However, all
	# `ContextualTokenEmbedders` could call `Contextualizer.forward(return_all_layers=True)`.

	# Second idea:

    def forward(sequence: torch.Tensor[batch, sequence, embedding]) -> torch.Tensor[batch, sequence, embedding]

	def get_layers(sequence: torch.Tensor) -> List[torch.Tensor[batch, sequence, embedding]]:

	# A separate method which basically implements the functionality above.
	# This would get around the type problems of `forward` possibly returning lists
	# of tensors when used as a `Seq2SeqEncoder`.
```

This doesn't handle Nelson's case that the contextualizers might be stateful during training. I haven't thought hard about that because i'm not 100% convinced that we need to support it as part of a concrete API yet, but i'm happy to be convinced!

```
python setup.py egg_info  # Create a requirements file.
```

done!

> also, can we have a brief natural language description of the algo?

It starts by computing the absolute difference of the means between the two samples, `reference_stat`. Then it starts drawing random permutations from the combined set x and y. Under the null hypothesis, the difference between these means are at least as extreme as the difference of the means between `x` and `y`. The p-value in this test represents the probability that we get results that are at least as extreme as `reference_stat`. We reject the null hypothesis that the two samples come from the same distributions if `p < 0.05`.

I don't quite like the new logic here, but I don't like the old logic as well 😅
The new one use a lot of apis inside workflow_storage which originally we want to hide from up layer.

The old one coupled storage with app layer a lot.

We probably can think about the positioning of `workflow_storage` a little bit.

Let's revisit what we have right now (with this PR):

* storage: an abstraction for database
* workflow_storage: contains the schema for workflow and use storage to write
  * key for different things
  * logic for how to updating things for different cases
* serialization: how to convert objects to bytes + dumps

I feel once we have open in workflow_storage, we'll decouple serialization/workflow_storage (right now, we need _key_ and also serialization.dump_to_storage() to make things work.

Then the next thing is about how to reduce the application-related code in workflow storage. One thing we can do is to move them to the place where they are used like what's doing here. 

So basically, we are going to have:

* storage: an abstraction for database
* workflow_storage: contains the schema for workflow build on top of the storage
* serialization: how to convert objects to bytes, like a pickler.
* application logic will be put in the application layer. and use serialization + workflow_storage to support specific functions

@sandcha @sarafalamaki At the risk of not answering the question directly, and being less helpful than I like, there's been a lot of previous discussion on the problems and limitations of parsing Python to figure out what variables a formula depends on; that was part of the point of my [proposal to reform formula syntax](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/issues/890) towards something more declarative.

This PR has a clever way of achieving the desired end result, using `co_consts` on the bytecode; it will do so with an accuracy that's difficult to estimate, but to hazard a guess I'd expect around 80-90% - this will be more than enough for relatively new country packages that don't suffer from the accumulated years of cruft of e.g. Openfisca-France, but it's bound to fail occasionally on a variety of edge cases and so I'd expect it to turn into a maintenance headache, to some degree.

My best advice would be not to do this and instead invest first in the proposed changes to formula syntax. I realize that this may be unpalatable advice, and I can understand if the absence of this feature is a deal-breaker for using Openfisca at all.

The second best course of action is to only do this if you're fully prepared to commit time and money to a roadmap for improving formula syntax such that statically computing formula dependencies can be done with 100% accuracy. In that case, yes, this PR could be seen as "prototyping the API contract for retrieving variable dependencies", with the option to then swap out the approximate bytecode-based implementation for an accurate, "architecture-based" one.

Be aware, such a choice comes with a serious risk - that of fooling oneself into thinking "we'll do the quick thing now and get around to cleaning up the technical debt later", and then, as one does, never getting around to cleaning up later. That is the very substance of technical debt.

Thanks for the PR!

This change might seem innocuous but it gets complicated pretty quickly. A couple of things: firstly, to enable using a different event loop implementation I would much prefer that `run()` receive a new parameter called `event_loop_policy`:

```
def run(
   ...
   event_loop_policy: Optional[AbstractEventLoopPolicy] = None
)
```

And then somewhere in the body you would check for `None` and then assign the default policy if none was specified, as I've done for the uvloop-versus-default handling.  This also means that some logic would be required to deal with what happens if a caller specifies both `use_uvloop` as well as a custom policy. Probably raise an error if both are set.  The code is already quite messy with checking all the combinations of boolean flags.

tbh I regret the `use_uvloop` parameter - I should have added `event_loop_policy` parameter instead and just let callers specify it themselves. Then aiorun wouldn't need to check inside the code for incompatible groups of options.  Too difficult to remove the `use_uvloop` parameter now probably, unless a very long deprecation cycle is used.

Last, and most importantly, there were quite a few upstream fixes applied to the proactor loop implementation between Python 3.5 and Python 3.8. There are reasons the proactor loop only became the default in 3.8!  Thus I am hesitant to change the default on windows to proactor on earlier python versions - even the core devs didn't want to and I don't know what kind of bugginess I'd be exposing myself to if we used the proactor loop in earlier python versions.  Even now, in 3.8 there are some nasty bugs in the proactor implementation (ever see exceptions about "overlapped futures" during shutdown? that's one of them).

So my proposal to move forward is

- add a new parameter to `run()` called event_loop_policy, default to None)
- check for incompatible groups of parameters, like if `use_uvloop` or `loop` is provided as well as a policy (and maybe other incompatible parameters alonside a policy?), and raise an error
- Continue to set `DefaultEventLoopPolicy` as the default (don't set proactor to the default on all python versions); this allows users to specify the proactor policy themselves on earlier versions if they choose.

This is more work than you might have been willing to do, it's fine by me if you don't want to do it.

@bilderbuchi thanks a lot for your review. Let me recap the target of modifications also to double check my understanding is correct.
1. We are trying to define a mechanism to allow instruments developer's to easily define default values in presence of multiple interfaces and when these default values are different for each interface
2. The only case is `VISAAdapter` currently managed by `pyvisa`.
3. We need to apply the default only when user's provide instrument address as a number or string since these are the only cases where `Instrument` class creates a `VISAAdapter` class for you.
4. If user passes an adapter class as instrument address, no default should be set (and in any case all kwargs are ignored)

Consequences:

1. If the instrument has only one interface or the default apply to all interfaces then `kwargs.setdefault` should do the job, this is just python no special support.
2. I proposed to have an helper function to set the defaults for instrument developers, I do not see any use of it for regular users

3. Since this make sense only for `VISAAdapter`, I defined it as a static method of such class. This helper function needs to know:

- the instrument address to understand which will be the interface type associated
- the interface type for which the defaults needs to be applied, this is just a number.
- the user provided keywords
- the default definitions via keyword parameters

Again this is an helper function and not a proper method of `VISAAdapter`, so it is meant to be used outside a class instance and actually it has no use for class instances.
This helper function is, in my opinion, more than adequate to handle corner cases of instruments with multiple interfaces which needs specific defaults on some of them. It could handle also all the `pyvisa` interfaces and not just `aslr` and `gpib`

You proposed method will require to add a specific parameter for each interface supported by `VISAAdapter` which in turn are the interfaces supported by `pyvisa`.

Finally, let me share my personal preference regarding the possible solution to this problem (in order of priority):
1. Just remove safeKeywords and nothing else. We are left with marginal problems that can be solved by users by definining their own adapter instances. For example, as it is today the Fluke7341 will fail, if a GPIB address is used. I know how to fix this, but it is a bit clunky.
2. Use the helper function  `VISAAdapter.setdefaults`, same as 1. but more elegant.
3. Add 10 parameters to `Instrument.__init__` to handle currently supported pyvisa interface `[ <InterfaceType.gpib: 1>, <InterfaceType.vxi: 2>, <InterfaceType.gpib_vxi: 3>, <InterfaceType.asrl: 4>, <InterfaceType.pxi: 5>, <InterfaceType.tcpip: 6>, <InterfaceType.usb: 7>, <InterfaceType.rio: 8>, <InterfaceType.firewire: 9>, <InterfaceType.rsnrp: 33024>]`. Obviously we can safely assume that no instrument offer all of them simultaneously, some interfaces I have no idea of what they are and they could be filtered out, but again why taking decision for the users which offer only risks, i.e. preventing to define defaults for the `rio` (whatever it is) interface, just to discover that we can create inconvenience to some users (similar issues created by `safeKeyword` approach).

Let me say also that I like very much the review process because offer the possibility to share valuable opinion between people with different background and culture.

```python
In [22]: sys
Out[22]: 
['The dog bit the man.',
 "It wasn't surprising.",
 'The man had just bitten him.']

In [23]: refs
Out[23]: 
[['The dog bit the man.', 'It was not unexpected.', 'The man bit him first.'],
 ['The dog had bit the man.',
  'No one was surprised.',
  'The man had bitten the dog.']]

In [24]: empty_sys
Out[24]: ['', '', '']

In [25]: sacrebleu.corpus_bleu(sys, refs)
Out[25]: BLEU = 48.53 82.4/50.0/45.5/37.5 (BP = 0.943 ratio = 0.944 hyp_len = 17 ref_len = 18)

In [26]: sacrebleu.corpus_bleu(empty_sys, refs)
```

Using a sentinel like is a habit of mine, which I use in these situations. Mainly for consistency because sometimes you want to also flag something as having "no default set" while still being able to store the value "None". And if I use `None` to flag something as having "no default" I can no longer determine whether the value was supposed to be `None` or whether I should use the default value. It also keeps things more explicit in code. Reading something like `if valus is NO_DEFAULT` communicates the intent better than reading `if value is None` (IMO). 

Considering that all the values here are booleans, we could use `None` as this special marker.

`notify_work_completed` is the `PhysicalOperator` API for "this future that you wanted the executor is done"; this includes both task futures AND worker is ready futures, and is an intentional part of the `Executor` <--> `PhysicalOperator` API and contract (notice that it's `notify_work_completed()` and `get_work_refs()`, not `notify_task_completed()` and `get_task_refs()`).

The current contract is: a `PhysicalOperator` provides a list of futures that it wants to executor's control loop to wait on (`op.get_work_refs()`), expecting the executor to tell the operator when each is done (`op.notify_work_completed()`). The executor doesn't care what these futures are, and the `PhysicalOperator` can have those futures represent any work it needs to carry out, as long as it can be waited on with `ray.wait()`: this can be an executing task, actor starting up, a placement group being scheduled, or even waiting for something external to the Ray cluster to be ready. The `ActorPoolMapOperator` exposes both running task futures and pending actor futures in `op.get_work_refs()`, and delineates between the two when a ref is received in `op.notify_work_completed()`.

We could have a separate `op.get_worker_refs()` and `op.notify_worker_started()` APIs, but that's exposing unnecessary detail to the `Executor`, who doesn't need to know those kind of details about the futures that its waiting on.

```
deadline = time.time() + timeout
for addr in getaddrinfo():
    [...]
    sock.settimeout(max(0, deadline - time.time()))
```

This way the timeout is accurate no matter how many address we try, and whether they succeed or not.

There are some import conventions (see [flake8-import-conventions](https://github.com/joaopalmeiro/flake8-import-conventions)), such as:

```python
import numpy as np
```

in real-world use cases, it should be:

```python
def func(x: float: np.inf) -> None: ...
```

The NumPy constants can be replaced with `math.*`:

```python
In [1]: import numpy as np

In [2]: import math

In [3]: np.inf == math.inf
Out[3]: True

In [4]: np.pi == math.pi
Out[4]: True
```

Since `*.pyi` are separate files other than the source code `*.py`. I think it's OK to only support `math.*` here.

As a user, i might want to alert on total GC time spent, for instance, per minute. If my application spends more than 5 seconds in GC over 2 minutes, i want to know about it. Splitting GC time into generations here would make it very complicated to calculate the sum of time spent in GC across generations. We'd essentially create a metrics stream per generation. To get the sum of them, you'd need to do a derivative for each one and then take the sum of them. If you're graphing them, a stacked graph might do, but for alerting, this is complicated.

It might be worth considering reporting the time spent in GC since the last collection instead. This is similar to the discussion we had on aggregated views (reporting deltas since collection versus ever increasing counters). I know the Java agent already shipped with other metrics, but i think it's worth having the discussion. ccing @felixbarny for input

With these changes, the "processing" of TrainingResults are happening in different places: reports are processed (yielded) in `next`, while checkpoints are processed in either `_fetch_next_result` or `_finish_checkpointing`.

Is it possible to refactor this a bit so that all of the processing is happening in one place?

One possible implementation:

So we can have one method that just obtains the next `TrainingResult` from the actor:
```
def get_next_result(self) -> Optional[List[TrainingResult]]
```

And then we can move most of the logic in `next` (or use some other helper method)
```python
def _get_next_result(self) -> Optional[List[TrainingResult]]:
	results = ray.get(self._executor.get_next_results.remote())
	return results

def _pause_reporting(self):
	ray.get(self._executor.pause_reporting.remote())

def _finish_training(self):
	# Assumes that all reporting and checkpointing are already finished.
	ray.get(self._executor.finish_training.remote())


def __next__(self):
	while True:
		if self.is_finished():
			raise StopIteration

		next_results = self._run_with_error_handling(self._get_next_result)

		if next_results is None:
			# There are no more reports or checkpoints.
			# So we don't need to pause reporting here.
			try:
				self._final_results = self._run_with_error_handling(
	                    self._finish_training)
			finally:
				self._finished_training = True
		else:
			first_result = next_results[0]
			result_type = first_result.type

			if result_type is TrainingResultType.REPORT:
                result_data = [r.data for r in results]
                yield result_data
            elif result_type is TrainingResultType.CHECKPOINT:
                self._checkpoint_manager._process_checkpoint(results)
                # Iterate until next REPORT call or training has finished.
            else:
                raise TrainBackendError(f"Unexpected result type: "
                                        f"{result_type}. "
                                        f"Expected one of "
                                        f"{[type in TrainingResultType]}")


def get_final_results(self, force) -> List[T]:
	if not self.is_finished():
        assert self._final_results is None
        if force:
        	# Pause reporting.
        	self._run_with_error_handling(self.pause_reporting)
            # Iterate and process remaining checkpoints.
            # This will also set self._final_results and self._finished_training
            for _ in self:
            	pass
            assert self.is_finished
        else:
        	logger.info("Please finish iterating through the "
                            "intermediate results before getting the"
                            "final returns. If you would like "
                            "training to finish immediately and get "
                            "the final returns, then set "
                            "`force=True`.")
    
    return self._final_results
```

Yes, computation of the signed area of a polygon is based on the Wolfram MathWorld solution.

Unfortunately `numy.linalg.det` works for square matrices. Because the vertices coordinates matrix is rectangular it would required to be reshaped and each row duplicated in order to use the determinant approach.

The solution from MathWorld works here because selfoverlapping polygons, while being self intersecting, does not contain _twists_ by definition. A twisted polygon will have two faces pointing upwards instead of single one. One of the faces with clockwise direction and the other in counterclockwise, causing the signed areas to substract instead of add, regardless of their sign. That could cause the area of a twisted polygon to be _0_ even when it clearly has area _greater than 0_.

Thanks for this! This might be cheating but I am currently using `Neovim` which is a `modern` Vim fork. I followed similar steps to register a macro and then execute it for every instance in one go. This is how I did it in Vim, there could be better ways of doing this, but I am not currently very aware of it (I started shifting my workflow to `Neovim`, only like a week back)
```
qa                          "Start recording a macro and store it in the register 'a'
/.rpad_and_clip(            "Find the first instance of "rpad_and_clip(" from the current line
f_                          "jump to the first "_"
de                          "delete till end of the work i.e _and_clip
%                           "go to the matching closing parenthesis i.e )
<InsertMode>                       
, clip=True                 "Insert the kwarg
<EscToNormalMode>
q                           "Stop recording the macro
```
Then we just do a `@a` for the first time and then just spam `@@` for every subsequent macro execution. It's pretty neat. For like proper modern IDEs / Text Editors, it becomes a bit difficult. I am not sure if there's a better way, but in VS Code, you'll have to write some editor specific commands in a JSON object and install an [extension](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=geddski.macros) to get the macro to work.

@korycins and I are wondering whether this should be changed to accept `net_amount_field`, `gross_amount_field` and `currency_field` instead of the current structure that forces you to create three virtual fields when you only need one.

Imagine the situation as described in the readme:

```python

class Model(models.Model):
    currency = models.CharField(max_length=3, default="BTC")
    price_net_amount = models.DecimalField(max_digits=9, decimal_places=2, default="5")
    price_net = MoneyField(amount_field="price_net_amount", currency_field="currency")
    price_gross_amount = models.DecimalField(
        max_digits=9, decimal_places=2, default="5"
    )
    price_gross = MoneyField(
        amount_field="price_gross_amount", currency_field="currency"
    )
    price = TaxedMoneyField(net_field='price_net', gross_field='price_gross')
```

```python
x = Model()
x.price_net = Money(5, 'USD')
x.price_gross = Money(7, 'EUR')
print(x.price)
y = Model(price_net=Money(1, 'PLN'), price_gross=(2, 'EUR'))

```
